text: This case illustrates a totally robotic pancreatic odoidinectomy in a seventy-year-old woman.
The patient was referred from another hospital, where she presented with complaints of intermittent jaundice.
ERCP was performed which showed stones in the common bile duct and gallbladder.
She underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
However, presented another episode of jaundice.
MRI showed a dilated bile duct without evidence of tumor.
The pancreatic duct is observed to be normal, measuring less than two millimeters in diameter.
No anatomic variations were found.
She was subsequently submitted to echoendoscopy, that showed residual stones and a small tumor in the distal bile duct.
Biopsy suggested malignancy.
The surgeon places ports on the patient's abdomen, preparing for laparoscopic access to perform the pancreatectomy and pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon adjusts the placement of trocars to ensure adequate working space and minimize collision between robotic arms during the procedure.
The surgeon lifts the stomach and maintains its position, providing clear access to the underlying structures for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon divides the omentum along the greater curvature of the stomach, separating the omental tissue from the gastric surface.
The surgeon exposes the anterior face of the pancreas and continues dissection towards the right, revealing the pancreatic tissue and surrounding structures.
The surgeon detaches the right colon and lowers it down, creating space for further dissection in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum, revealing the underlying vena cava, with clear visualization of the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon performs dissection of the lymph nodes along the hepatic artery, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects and encircles the gastroduodenal artery, carefully isolating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a clamping test on the pancreas, followed by dividing the pancreas between two clips.
The surgeon dissects the common bile duct, clamps it with a bulldog clamp, and then divides the duct, completing the separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the pilar lymphadenectomy, removing lymphatic tissue from the area surrounding the pancreas and duodenum.
The surgeon controls and divides the gastroepiploic vessels, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the duodenum, making the cut two centimeters below the pylorus.
The surgeon performs further dissection along the portal vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon passes an umbilical tape around the neck of the pancreas, encircling the structure for further manipulation or retraction.
The surgeon carefully detaches the superior mesenteric vein from the uncinate process, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon uses clips to control small venous branches, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes the uncinate process of the pancreas, separating it from surrounding structures to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon uses harmonic shears to divide the pancreas, carefully progressing with the active blade until the pancreatic duct is visually identified.
The surgeon divides the pancreatic duct using scissors, leaving a visible stump suitable for subsequent anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the transection of the pancreas, ensuring the separation of the pancreatic tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon mobilizes the ligament of Treitz and passes the jejunum behind the mesenteric vessels, demonstrating careful manipulation of the intestinal structures.
The surgeon uses a surgical stapler to transect the bowel, visibly separating the intestinal tissue with precision.
The surgeon lifts the pancreatic head and carefully dissects the uncinate process from the portal vein and superior mesenteric artery, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon identifies the inferior pancreatic artery and divides it between clips, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The portal vein and superior mesenteric artery are fully skeletonized, with surrounding tissue meticulously dissected away to expose these vascular structures.
The completion of the pancreaticoduodenectomy is visually confirmed, with the surgical field showing the resected pancreatic head and duodenum, and the remaining structures in their final positions.
The surgeon performs a pancreaticojejunostomy using the duct-to-mucosa technique, visually aligning the pancreatic duct with the jejunal mucosa for anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the posterior layer of the anastomosis using interrupted sutures with 4-0 Prolene, securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon performs a duct-to-mucosa anastomosis using interrupted V-O sutures, carefully aligning the pancreatic duct with the intestinal mucosa.
A pediatric feeding tube is placed as an internal stent within the surgical site.
The surgeon completes the anterior part of the anastomosis, finalizing the connection between the pancreatic duct and the intestinal mucosa.
The surgeon removes the Bulldog clamp from the common bile duct, allowing for the restoration of bile flow following the completion of the anastomosis.
The surgeon creates an opening in the jejunum using harmonic shears, preparing the site for further anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy, utilizing a foro-continuous suture technique to connect the hepatic duct to the jejunum.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a test to confirm there is no visible bile leakage from the surgical site.
The surgeon performs an anticholic duodenogygenostomy, connecting the duodenum to the jejunum, ensuring proper alignment and secure suturing of the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs the posterior layer of the anastomosis using a 3-0 running suture, securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon opens the stomach and jejunum, creating visible incisions in both structures.
The surgeon completes a full-thickness anastomosis using a running 3-0 absorbable suture, ensuring secure tissue approximation.
The surgeon completes the anterior layer of the anastomosis using interrupted sutures, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon retrieves the surgical specimen inside a plastic bag through the umbilical port, following the enlargement of the incision.
The frozen section analysis reveals clear surgical margins, indicating no residual tumor presence at the resection site.
The distal bile duct is exposed, revealing a visible tumor within the duct.
Operative time was seven hours.
The surgical field shows minimal bleeding, with no visible need for transfusion during the procedure.
Recovery was uneventful except for delayed gastric emptying.
Final pathology confirmed cholangiocarcinoma T-N-Zero.
